name: Lange v. California
summary: A highway patrol officer followed a suspected misdemeanant into his attached garage and conducted an investigation inside the home, without a warrant, on the theory that pursuit of a fleeing suspect is always an exigent circumstance. The Court unanimously rejected a categorical rule and held that officers must assess whether actual exigency exists before entering a home in pursuit of a misdemeanor suspect.
holding: The pursuit of a suspected misdemeanant does not categorically authorize warrantless entry into a home. Officers may enter without a warrant only where the specific circumstances create a genuine exigency — such as imminent danger, threatened destruction of evidence, or risk of escape — not merely because a suspect retreated into a home after a minor offense.
relevance_to_audit: Limits warrantless home entries in misdemeanor pursuit; relevant to enforcement programs that use surveillance technology to identify and pursue low-level offenders into residential areas.

key_quotes.text: The flight of a suspected misdemeanant does not always—or even usually—create the exigent circumstances that can justify a warrantless home entry.
key_quotes.context: Rejecting the government's proposed categorical rule that any hot pursuit justifies warrantless home entry.

procedural_posture: California Court of Appeal affirmed conviction; reversed and remanded
subsequent_treatment: Applied to limit warrantless entries in administrative enforcement contexts; Kavanaugh and Roberts concurrences suggest narrower application
